retrieval_and_qa.answer:
The document presents Endeavor as “the nonprofit founded by Linda Rottenberg and Peter Kellner,” framed around “entrepreneurship for emerging markets, especially Latin America,” and later describes it as operating “as a multiplier system as much as an accelerator.” [CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:0|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=0|page=1|start_char=0|end_char=992] [CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:8|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=8|page=2|start_char=6400|end_char=7396]

Argentina is described as one of the clearest stages for this network, and Endeavor Argentina is said to have drawn in Marcos Galperin of MercadoLibre along with other prominent Argentine founders and business figures. [CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:2|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=2|page=1|start_char=1600|end_char=2597]

On MercadoLibre specifically, the document says Galperin’s company became “the most visible success story,” describing it as “an e-commerce and payments giant often compared to Amazon and eBay,” with MercadoPago extending its reach into “digital payments and crypto-adjacent finance.” [CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:2|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=2|page=1|start_char=1600|end_char=2597] [CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:3|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=3|page=1|start_char=2389|end_char=3385]

The document also says the article tied MercadoLibre to “Paxos, PYUSD, Ripio, Mercado Bitcoin, and a wider payments-and-stablecoin corridor running through Argentina and Brazil.” [CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:3|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=3|page=1|start_char=2389|end_char=3385]

More broadly, the narrative says a founder could move through Endeavor Argentina, raise capital, partner with firms like eBay or PayPal, and later sit on boards linked to companies including MercadoLibre, which places MercadoLibre within Endeavor’s wider cross-border network of founders, investors, and institutions. [CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:8|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=8|page=2|start_char=6400|end_char=7396]

retrieval_and_qa.retrieval_query_contract:
WITH node AS c, score
WHERE c.run_id = $run_id
  AND ($source_uri IS NULL OR c.source_uri = $source_uri)
RETURN c.text AS chunk_text,
       c.chunk_id AS chunk_id,
       c.run_id AS run_id,
       c.source_uri AS source_uri,
       c.chunk_index AS chunk_index,
       coalesce(c.page_number, c.page) AS page,
       c.start_char AS start_char,
       c.end_char AS end_char,
       score AS similarityScore

retrieval_results.content:
broader PayPal mafia, became another recurring figure. Around them were institutions
such as the Council on Foreign Relations, the World Economic Forum, the Aspen Institute, Bain Capital,
Goldman Sachs, J.P . Morgan, eBay, PayPal, and In-Q-tel. The story moved easily between philanthropy,
venture capital, public policy, and digital infrastructure, creating the impression that Endeavor was less a
simple nonprofit than a relay point linking financiers, tech executives, political operators, and ambitious
founders across the Americas.
Argentina became one of the clearest stages for this network. Endeavor Argentina drew in Marcos Galperin
of MercadoLibre, Guibert Englebienne and Martin Migoya of Globant, Maria Julia Bearzi, Eduardo Elzstain,
and later figures orbiting Javier Milei’s business-friendly circles. Galperin’s MercadoLibre became the most
visible  success  story:  an  e-commerce  and  payments  giant  often  compared  to  Amazon  and  eBay,  with
MercadoPago extending its reach 
[CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:2|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=2|page=1|start_char=1600|end_char=2597]

ork, and the World Economic Forum sat near founders
building payments companies, crypto exchanges, AI consultancies, and e-commerce platforms. For a graph
database demo, that mixture is useful because a user can jump from a person to an organization, from an
organization to an investor , from an investor to a country, and from a country to a sector such as crypto, AI,
logistics, or digital identity.
Read as a condensed narrative, the story suggests that Endeavor operated as a multiplier system as much
as an accelerator . A founder might begin in Buenos Aires, meet mentors through Endeavor Argentina, raise
capital from investors in New York or San Francisco, sell to Santander , partner with eBay or PayPal, appear
at the Llao Llao Forum with Eduardo Elzstain and Javier Milei, and later sit on boards linked to Microsoft,
MercadoLibre, Globant, Xapo, Ripio, Kaszek, or Tether-connected ventures. Linda Rottenberg, Peter Kellner ,
Edgar Bronfman Jr ., Pierre Omidyar , Reid Hoffman, Marcos 
[CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:8|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=8|page=2|start_char=6400|end_char=7396]

business-friendly circles. Galperin’s MercadoLibre became the most
visible  success  story:  an  e-commerce  and  payments  giant  often  compared  to  Amazon  and  eBay,  with
MercadoPago extending its reach into digital payments and crypto-adjacent finance. The article also tied
MercadoLibre  to  Paxos,  PYUSD,  Ripio,  Mercado  Bitcoin,  and  a  wider  payments-and-stablecoin  corridor
running  through  Argentina  and  Brazil.  Nearby  sat  Banco  Hipotecario  and  IRSA,  both  associated  with
Elzstain, along with the Llao Llao Forum, where business elites, startup founders, and political figures
gathered in Patagonia to discuss cryptocurrency, artificial intelligence, and generational change.
Another  branch  of  the  story  ran  through  Wences  Casares,  Patagon,  and  later  Xapo.  Endeavor  helped
arrange roadshows that introduced Casares to investors in New York, Boston, and San Francisco, and
Patagon was later sold to Santander . In this compressed version, Casares looks 
[CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:3|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=3|page=1|start_char=2389|end_char=3385]

The Endeavor Network: A Simplified Narrative for
Graph Demo Ingestion
Condensed unstructured sample adapted from the attached source article
Demo note: This version is intentionally simplified and lightly fictionalized for entity extraction, relationship
extraction, and graph retrieval testing. It preserves many of the people, companies, places, and institutional
links mentioned in the source while keeping a narrative, article-like style.
In  the  late  1990s,  a  network  began  to  take  shape  around  Endeavor ,  the  nonprofit  founded  by  Linda
Rottenberg and Peter Kellner . The project was framed as entrepreneurship for emerging markets, especially
Latin America, but the cast around it quickly stretched from Buenos Aires and Santiago to New York,
Washington, Silicon Valley, and Davos. Rottenberg, who had worked in Argentina and moved through circles
connected  to  Ashoka  and  Bill  Drayton,  presented  Endeavor  as  a  bridge  for  founders  who  were  too
ambitious for 
[CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:0|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=0|page=1|start_char=0|end_char=992]

pillar to the network. Founded in Buenos Aires, it grew into a software, AI, and
digital transformation company serving clients such as Google, Disney, Electronic Arts, Nestle, American
Express, and Santander Bank. Martin Migoya spoke about preparing institutions for a digital and cognitive
future, while the company’s leadership appeared beside Endeavor , the Council of the Americas, Microsoft,
and major banks. In the source material, Globant did not stand alone; it shared a stage with MercadoLibre
and  with  public-sector  modernization,  turning  the  company  into  a  useful  node  for  demos  about  how
corporate service firms sit between governments, platforms, cloud vendors, and the AI boom. A graph built
from this material could connect Globant not just to Argentina but to California, New York, London, and
Madrid through clients, investors, directors, and policy networks.
The  cast  widened  further  with  Kiva,  Better  Than  Cash  Alliance,  the  United  Nations,  the  
[CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:5|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=5|page=2|start_char=3999|end_char=4991]

Davos. Rottenberg, who had worked in Argentina and moved through circles
connected  to  Ashoka  and  Bill  Drayton,  presented  Endeavor  as  a  bridge  for  founders  who  were  too
ambitious for microfinance and too far from old family money. Kellner , who moved through Princeton,
Harvard  Business  School,  Yale  Law  School,  and  the  Aspen  orbit,  helped  turn  that  idea  into  a  durable
network of mentors, investors, board members, and favored companies.
As  Endeavor  expanded,  names  from  elite  finance  and  technology  kept  appearing  around  it.  Edgar
Bronfman Jr . became a central force in shaping the group’s board and strategy. Pierre Omidyar and the
Omidyar  Network  supplied  major  funding.  Reid  Hoffman,  connected  to  PayPal,  LinkedIn,  Greylock,
Microsoft, and the broader PayPal mafia, became another recurring figure. Around them were institutions
such as the Council on Foreign Relations, the World Economic Forum, the Aspen Institute, Bain Capital,
Goldman 
[CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:1|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=1|page=1|start_char=796|end_char=1795]

on boards linked to Microsoft,
MercadoLibre, Globant, Xapo, Ripio, Kaszek, or Tether-connected ventures. Linda Rottenberg, Peter Kellner ,
Edgar Bronfman Jr ., Pierre Omidyar , Reid Hoffman, Marcos Galperin, Martin Migoya, Wences Casares, Maria
Julia Bearzi, Guibert Englebienne, Sebastián Serrano, and Emiliano Kargieman all become retrievable nodes
in  the  same  narrative  web.  That  is  what  makes  the  document  suitable  for  LLM  extraction  and  graph
retrieval:  it  is  messy,  unstructured,  name-dense,  geographically  distributed,  and  full  of  repeated
relationships across people, companies, boards, conferences, investors, and states.
2
Suggested entity targets in this sample include people, companies, investors, countries, cities, nonprofits,
forums,  government  bodies,  financial  institutions,  products,  and  technologies.  Useful  relations  include
founded,  funded,  advised,  partnered_with,  sat_on_board_of,  invested_in,  based_in,  operated_in,
connected_to, 
[CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:9|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=9|page=2|start_char=7199|end_char=8197]

helped
arrange roadshows that introduced Casares to investors in New York, Boston, and San Francisco, and
Patagon was later sold to Santander . In this compressed version, Casares looks like a hinge connecting
Argentine entrepreneurship, Wall Street capital, Silicon Valley prestige, and the early mythology of Bitcoin
custody.  The  same  narrative  current  also  carried  Sebastián  Serrano  of  Ripio,  Rodrigo  Benzaquen  of
Moneero,  Andy  Freire,  Nicolas  Szekasy,  and  Kaszek  Ventures,  making  the  Southern  Cone  feel  like  an
1
unusually  dense  cluster  of  founders,  venture  vehicles,  and  crypto  infrastructure  firms.  Even  when  the
companies  were  different,  the  board  members,  mentors,  sponsors,  and  conference  circuits  kept
overlapping.
Globant added a second pillar to the network. Founded in Buenos Aires, it grew into a software, AI, and
digital transformation company serving clients such as Google, Disney, Electronic Arts, Nestle, American
Express, and 
[CITATION|chunk_id=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf:4|run_id=unstructured_ingest-20260415T204704651227Z-5d9ae4ea|source_uri=file:///Users/ash/Documents/repos/Zoomlytics/power-atlas/demo/fixtures/datasets/demo_dataset_v1/unstructured/chain_of_custody.pdf|chunk_index=4|page=1|start_char=3200|end_char=4197]

California, New York, London, and
Madrid through clients, investors, directors, and policy networks.
The  cast  widened  further  with  Kiva,  Better  Than  Cash  Alliance,  the  United  Nations,  the  Rockefeller
Foundation, the Gates Foundation, Co-Develop, and digital identity efforts in places such as Sierra Leone. In
the simplified telling, these names form another layer of the same map: fintech for the unbanked, digital
IDs, blockchain rails, and payment modernization in the Global South. Omidyar’s ecosystem linked eBay and
PayPal to later nonprofit and policy initiatives, while Hoffman linked venture capital, political influence, and
platform power . The article also mentioned Howard Cox, George Tenet, Mayo Shattuck III, Larry Summers,
and other figures who made the boundary between finance, intelligence, and technology feel porous,
especially  when  companies  such  as  In-Q-tel-backed  firms,  Palantir-adjacent  networks,  or  surveillance-
oriented platforms appeared in the

By the time the narrative reached more recent years, the map included satellites, stablecoins, and national
politics. Emiliano Kargieman’s Satellogic appeared with Steve Mnuchin, Joe Dunford, Howard Lutnick, Tether
Investments,  Brock  Pierce,  and  Cantor  Fitzgerald.  The  regional  conversation  touched  Argentina,  Brazil,
Uruguay, Chile, Mexico, and the broader Latin American market, but it also kept circling back to New York,
Washington,  Silicon  Valley,  Israel,  and  global  institutions.  Figures  associated  with  the  World  Jewish
Congress, AIPAC, the Aspen Global Leadership Network, and the World Economic Forum sat near founders
building payments companies, crypto exchanges, AI consultancies, and e-commerce platforms. For a graph
database demo, that mixture is useful because
